items.question: Who uses batch gradient descent in practice?
items.answer: Students, researchers prototyping on small datasets, and engineers debugging optimizers use full-batch GD; large-scale Gen AI training typically switches to mini-batch SGD but inherits the same update rule.

items.question: What is the batch gradient descent update rule?
items.answer: θ ← θ − η∇L(θ), where θ are parameters, η > 0 is the learning rate, and ∇L(θ) is the gradient of the average loss over the entire training set.

items.question: When is batch gradient descent appropriate?
items.answer: When the full dataset fits comfortably in memory and compute budget, and you want exact gradients with smooth per-epoch loss curves—for teaching, debugging or modest-scale regression and classification.

items.question: Where does gradient descent operate in a training pipeline?
items.answer: After forward pass and loss computation, autograd produces ∇L; the optimizer applies θ ← θ − η∇L(θ) to every trainable parameter before the next epoch or step.

items.question: Why subtract η∇L instead of adding it?
items.answer: ∇L points toward steepest loss increase; minimizing requires moving opposite that direction, so subtracting η∇L implements steepest local descent on the loss surface.

items.question: How does one epoch of batch GD differ from one mini-batch step?
items.answer: One batch-GD epoch computes ∇L over all training examples and updates θ once. Mini-batch training performs many smaller gradient estimates and updates per epoch, introducing noise but far lower per-step cost.

items.question: Cause: learning rate η is too large for the current loss curvature.
items.answer: Effect: updates overshoot the minimum—loss oscillates, diverges or cycles even with exact full-batch gradients.

items.question: Cause: gradients are not zeroed before a new backward pass.
items.answer: Effect: accumulated ∇L doubles effective step size, destabilizing θ ← θ − η∇L(θ) and producing erratic loss.

items.question: Cause: loss surface is non-convex with multiple valleys.
items.answer: Effect: batch GD converges to a local minimum dependent on initialization, not necessarily the global best parameters.
